lessons.title: Lesson 01: Pronouns, Greetings, and Basic Negation

summary.description_ru: На первом уроке курса преподаватель Дмитрий Петров рассказывает о том, что такое китайский язык, в чем заключаются его особенности. А ученики, в свою очередь, говорят о том, почему они решили изучать китайский, чем он их заинтересовал.
summary.extended_description_ru: На первом уроке курса преподаватель Дмитрий Петров рассказывает о том, что такое китайский язык, в чем заключаются его особенности. А ученики, в свою очередь, говорят о том, почему они решили изучать китайский, чем он их заинтересовал. Дмитрий Петров: "Китайский язык обладает рядом удивительных особенностей, которые, с одной стороны, делаю его более сложным, чем многие из знакомых нам языков. Но есть, конечно, и бонусы, потому что ни один народ в мире не придумывает назло иностранцам слишком сложный язык. Всё чем-то обосновано. Все особенности языка имеют какое-то происхождение. В китайском языке интересно то, что есть определённый набор элементов, который строго ограничен. Элементы эти могут быть либо словами, либо выполнять роль слога, когда слово состоит из нескольких таких частей. Количество таких элементов очень ограничено. Их примерно четыреста. И вот сколько существует китайский язык, количество этих элементов не меняется".
lesson_notes_ru: На первом уроке курса преподаватель Дмитрий Петров рассказывает о том, что такое китайский язык, в чем заключаются его особенности.
А ученики, в свою очередь, говорят о том, почему они решили изучать китайский, чем он их заинтересовал.
Дмитрий Петров: "Китайский язык обладает рядом удивительных особенностей, которые, с одной стороны, делаю его более сложным, чем многие из знакомых нам языков.
Но есть, конечно, и бонусы, потому что ни один народ в мире не придумывает назло иностранцам слишком сложный язык.

lesson_lines.translation_en: And now we can see where the familiar greeting comes from.
It means good, kind, or well; the distinction is not important here.
Literally it comes out as something like 'may things be good for you.'
So in the plural you literally say 'you all, good' or 'all the best to you all.'
Now, if we want to ask how someone is doing, this is what changes.
Ma is basically the question marker.
These particles do not carry standalone lexical meaning; 你好吗? just means 'How are you?'
To answer that, you say that you are well.
In Chinese people usually say not just 'fine' but 'very fine.'
Think of it like the Russian particle 'ли': 'Are you well?' and then 'I am very well.'
If you want to ask back 'And you?', there is another little particle for that.
Now, to get the possessive form, we add one more small particle.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 我的 / 你的 / 他的 / 好的
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo de / ni de / ta de / hao de
lesson_lines.translation_en: my / your / his or her / okay
lesson_lines.translation_ru: мой / твой / его или её / хорошо

lesson_lines.translation_en: We have already seen the verb 爱, 'to love.'
And if we add 吗, it stays neutral and turns the line into a yes-no question.
Ma is neutral and always goes at the end.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ta men bu ai wo men

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 我 / 你 / 他 / 她 / 我们 / 你们 / 他们
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo / ni / ta / ta / women / nimen / tamen
lesson_lines.translation_en: I / you / he / she / we / you all / they
lesson_lines.translation_ru: я / ты / он / она / мы / вы / они

lesson_lines.translation_en: So after this first hour we should keep the pronoun system, SVO order, negation with 不, questions with 吗, and the core etiquette phrases.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 你们好 / 你好吗？ / 我很好 / 谢谢 / 你呢？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni men hao / ni hao ma? / wo hen hao / xiexie / ni ne?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Hello, everyone. How are you? I am fine. Thank you. And you?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Здравствуйте всем. Как дела? У меня всё хорошо. Спасибо. А ты?

grammar_patterns.name: SVO word order
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Basic Chinese word order in the lesson is subject + verb + object, for example 我爱你.

grammar_patterns.name: No case endings on pronouns
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Pronouns keep the same form across subject and object roles in this lesson's examples.

grammar_patterns.name: Negation with 不
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The particle 不 goes before the verb to negate it, as in 不爱.

grammar_patterns.name: Yes/no questions with 吗
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Add 吗 at the end of a statement to turn it into a yes/no question.

grammar_patterns.name: Plural pronouns with 们
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson introduces plural pronouns by adding 们 to pronoun bases.

grammar_patterns.name: Possessive with 的
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Add 的 after a pronoun to make possessive forms such as 我的, 你的, and 他的.

grammar_patterns.name: A-not-A questions
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson also shows the compact yes-no pattern 爱不爱 for asking whether someone loves or not.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Practice third-tone sandhi: 你 is pronounced more like a rising tone before 好.
Keep the x sound light and fronted; the second syllable is shorter and lighter.
Keep both syllables clipped and clear; avoid turning 再见 into a Russian-style diphthong.
Treat 吗 as an unstressed question particle at the end of the sentence.
Keep 呢 very light; the weight stays on 你 and the whole phrase should sound short and conversational.

drills.prompt: Repeat the greeting exchange: 你好. 你好吗? 我很好.
coaching_notes: Keep 吗 unstressed and make 我很好 one smooth phrase.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I love you.
coaching_notes: Keep strict subject + verb + object order.

drills.prompt: Swap pronouns to say: You love me.
coaching_notes: Do not add case endings; just change pronouns and keep order.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: They do not love us.
coaching_notes: Place 不 before 爱.

drills.prompt: Roleplay a polite closing: thank the listener and say goodbye.
coaching_notes: Keep 谢谢 short and 再见 crisp.

tests.question: What does 你好吗？ mean?
Which particle turns a statement into a yes/no question in this lesson?
Translate into Chinese: And you?
Translate into Chinese: Thank you all.
Translate into Chinese: He loves you.

teaching_notes: Open with the etiquette loop first: 你好 -> 你好吗？ -> 我很好 -> 谢谢 -> 再见.
Keep the first grammar frame extremely narrow: pronouns + 爱 + object.
Use lesson 01 to automate word order and particles before adding more verbs.
Do not overload Hanzi writing yet; focus on recognition, pronunciation, and oral pattern control.

lessons.title: Lesson 02: Question Words, Identity, and Place

lesson_lines.translation_en: To consolidate the first lesson, today we make a more decisive jump into fuller speech.
Question words are key because dialogue is built from questions and answers.
Now the lesson adds 是, the copula used mainly to identify someone or something.
to be; to be identified as
The next etiquette phrase is the pattern for asking someone's name.
to be called; to be named
The fuller form is literally something like 'You are called what name?'
Chinese names are usually adapted rather than copied mechanically from Russian forms.
Frequent visitors to China often receive a Chinese name for everyday communication.
By this point the class can already say name, residence, and what they do.
Here 在 works like the location marker 'in' or 'at'.
Chinese speech keeps syllables distinct because unstressed reduction is much weaker than in Russian.
The element 人 is used to form many nationality, city, and profession labels.
To ask where someone lives, keep direct word order and add the location marker.
What country are you from? / What nationality are you?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Ты из какой страны? / Какой ты национальности?

lesson_lines.translation_en: Now the lesson shifts into profession words that matter for the people in this classroom.
Chinese profession words here do not change for gender.
The plural marker 们 is used with people, but it is not always required in every sentence.
Many profession words in this block end with 人 because they name a person who performs a role.
To ask about work, the lesson uses 做什么工作, literally 'do what work?'

lesson_lines.translation_ru: Кем ты работаешь? / Какую работу ты делаешь?

lesson_lines.translation_en: After this vocabulary block, the lesson moves into live practice with native-speaking guests.
Titles such as 老师 are placed after a family name when addressing someone.
What are you called? / What is your name?
Break the sentence into pieces first, then rebuild it fluently with the correct tones.
The teacher advises splitting long answers into short memorized chunks before recombining them.
The pattern literally asks which-country person you are.
Adding 吗 at the end turns the nationality statement into a yes-no question.
The same question pattern is reused with different nationalities and professions.
The class now drills Moscow again because every student can use it immediately.
Find a little time to cycle through pronouns plus professions and the new identity patterns.

vocabulary.translation_en: to be; to be identified as
to be called; to be named

grammar_patterns.name: Question words stay in slot
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Question words such as 什么, 谁, and 哪儿 stay in the same place as the information they replace.

grammar_patterns.name: Copula 是 for identity
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use 是 to identify who someone is, what nationality they are, or what profession they have.

grammar_patterns.name: Name pattern with 叫
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use 叫 to ask and answer names: 你叫什么名字? / 我叫....

grammar_patterns.name: Residence with 住在
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use 住 plus 在 plus a place to say where someone lives.

grammar_patterns.name: Nationality pattern with 哪国人
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Ask nationality with 你是哪国人 and answer with a country plus 人.

grammar_patterns.name: Job question with 做什么工作
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Ask about work with 做什么工作 and answer with a 是 + profession sentence.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Keep the second syllable in 什么 light and unstressed; the question word should not sound like two equal Russian stresses.
Separate 谁 and 是 clearly; Russian ears often blur these into one fuzzy sh-sound cluster.
Keep 哪儿 short and conversational inside 在哪儿; do not over-stress the final r-colouring.
Practice the tone contour lao3 shi1 as a stable address form after a surname.
Keep 工作 clipped and rhythmic; avoid flattening the first syllable into Russian-style stress timing.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: What is your name?
coaching_notes: Keep 叫 and 名字 separate and clear.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I live in Moscow.
coaching_notes: Use 住在 as one residence frame.

drills.prompt: Swap the country and ask: Are you Russian?
coaching_notes: Keep 吗 at the end and retain the identity frame with 是.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: What work do you do?
coaching_notes: Treat 做什么工作 as one stable question pattern.

drills.prompt: Roleplay a short self-introduction: name, city, nationality, and profession.
drills.target_answer: 我叫马克。我住在莫斯科。我是俄罗斯人。我是歌手。
coaching_notes: Use four short clauses instead of one long Russian-style sentence.

tests.question: What does 你住在哪儿？ mean?
Which word in lesson 02 means 'to be called; to be named'?
Translate into Chinese: What country are you from?
Translate into Chinese: I am a singer.
Translate into Chinese: Thank you, teacher.

teaching_notes: Open lesson 02 by reviewing only one grammar expansion at a time: question words, then name, then residence, then nationality, then work.
Keep student output in very short clauses before combining them into mini self-introductions.
Use profession words only if they matter to the learner; the lesson itself models this selective vocabulary strategy.
When drilling nationality and residence, keep the frames separate: 我住在... versus 我是...人.
Use 老师 as an address form after a surname only after learners can already control the tones.

lessons.title: Lesson 03: Core Verbs, Wants, and Food/Drink Choices

lesson_lines.translation_en: When the whole class answers together in Chinese, the language already begins to feel usable.
The teacher expects learners to combine the small set of words from the first two lessons into fresh sentences.
Up to this point the class has only had a small set of verbs.
Lesson 03 expands the active verb stock and then adds the numerals from one to ten.
to ask; to ask a question
Now the class flips the same pattern to say: you ask me.
The teacher now asks how to say: who are you looking at?
Word order stays stable: subject, verb, then the question slot.
With a question word like 谁, the lesson drops 吗 and asks directly about the missing information.
The next high-frequency verb is introduced after the look/watch drill.
Are you not listening to me?
The teacher pauses to explain that a small set of verbs covers most everyday speech.
The core verb set includes hearing, seeing, speaking, working, doing, eating, and drinking.
to want; to think; to miss
The same verb 想 can mean want, think, or miss depending on context.
The class starts using 想 for current desire: what do you want to drink?
What do you want to drink?
What do you want to eat?
What do you want to eat?

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo bu he cha. wo he kafei.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I do not drink tea. I drink coffee.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я не пью чай. Я пью кофе.

lesson_lines.translation_en: The class contrasts a simple factual statement about drinking coffee with a present desire to drink it.
For immediate desire, the lesson keeps 想 in front of the verb phrase.
I do not want to drink coffee.
The teacher now adds a politeness marker for requests.
By this point the lesson has shown that the same pronoun-and-verb frame works for several new verbs.
The teacher now isolates the one major negation exception: 有 does not negate with 不.
to not have; there is not
The lesson shifts from verbs into family vocabulary so students can talk about real people around them.
With close family members, 的 can often be dropped in everyday speech.
The first family nouns are mother and father.
The teacher turns the family nouns into a residence question about the father.
The next family block introduces children and siblings.
The teacher points back to 有 so the class can say what family members they have.
A student asks to try a spouse question with the new family words.
The class asks whether one sentence can say there is no husband and no children.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo mei you zhangfu, mei you haizi.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I do not have a husband, and I do not have children.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: У меня нет мужа и нет детей.

lesson_lines.translation_en: The teacher moves into live family conversation with native speakers.
The live practice topic is explicitly family.
The first live prompt asks whether the person has a father or a mother.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni mama ai he kafei ma?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Does your mother like drinking coffee?
The teacher pauses to explain how tones interact in 有 and 没有.
The conjunction 和 now lets the class join family nouns in a single phrase.
The lesson explicitly contrasts 和 'and' with 喝 'to drink' because the sound difference is tonal.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 我不爱喝咖啡。我爱喝茶。
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo bu ai he kafei. wo ai he cha.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I do not like drinking coffee. I like drinking tea.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я не люблю пить кофе. Я люблю пить чай.

lesson_lines.translation_en: The child-name question is unpacked before the student supplies the answer.
What does she like to drink?

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni you gege he didi ma?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Do you have an older brother and a younger brother?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: У тебя есть старший и младший брат?

lesson_lines.translation_en: My older brother is a doctor.
My mother is also a doctor.
My father is not a doctor.
The lesson closes by writing and pronouncing the numerals from one to ten.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 一，二，三，四，五，六，七，八，九，十。
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: yi, er, san, si, wu, liu, qi, ba, jiu, shi.
lesson_lines.translation_en: one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten
lesson_lines.translation_ru: один, два, три, четыре, пять, шесть, семь, восемь, девять, десять

lesson_lines.translation_en: The teacher closes by saying the class can already say and understand much more than at the start of the course.

vocabulary.translation_en: to want; to think; to miss

grammar_patterns.name: New verbs keep the same pronoun frame
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 03 shows that once the pronoun order is stable, new verbs such as 问, 看, and 听 drop into the same subject-verb-object pattern.

grammar_patterns.name: Content question versus 吗 question
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use question words like 谁 or 什么 when asking for missing information, and use 吗 only for yes-no questions.

grammar_patterns.name: 想 + verb + object
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To say what someone wants right now, place 想 before the action: 想喝, 想吃, or 想喝水.

grammar_patterns.name: 有 / 没有 for possession
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use 有 to say that someone has a family member and 没有 to say that they do not.

grammar_patterns.name: Family possession can omit 的
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: With close family members, 的 is often optional, so the lesson allows compact forms like 我妈妈.

grammar_patterns.name: Conjunction 和 links family nouns
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use 和 to join two family nouns or other nouns in one phrase.

grammar_patterns.name: Job question with 做什么工作
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The class reuses the work question from lesson 02 to ask about relatives' professions.

grammar_patterns.name: Numerals one to ten
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson ends by introducing the basic counting sequence from one through ten.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Keep the final nasal clear: 问 has a short -n ending, while 听 carries the longer -ng ending.
Keep 水 as one compact syllable ending in -ui; do not split it into two Russian-style beats.
The lesson highlights tone behavior around 有 and 没有, so practice them as a fixed pair rather than isolated syllables.
和 'and' and 喝 'drink' sound close to beginners, so keep them distinct by tone and sentence role.
The ending number block trains clean tone contrast between 一 and 十, which beginners often flatten together.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: What do you want to drink?
coaching_notes: Keep 想 directly before 喝 and end with the question word 什么.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I do not drink tea. I drink coffee.
coaching_notes: Use 不 only once per clause and restart the sentence for the contrast.

drills.prompt: Introduce your family in three short clauses using 有 or 没有.
drills.target_answer: 我有妈妈。我有爸爸。我没有孩子。
coaching_notes: Keep each family fact as a short separate clause before combining them.

drills.prompt: Ask in Chinese: Where does your family live?
coaching_notes: Keep 家 before 住 and leave 哪儿 in the place slot.

drills.prompt: Count from one to ten aloud with the teacher.
drills.target_answer: 一，二，三，四，五，六，七，八，九，十。
coaching_notes: Keep every number separate and do not rush the middle of the sequence.

tests.question: What does 你看谁？ mean?
Translate into Chinese: I want to drink water.
Which pair expresses have / do not have in lesson 03?
Translate into Chinese: My father is not a doctor.
Count from one to ten in Chinese.
tests.answer_key: 一，二，三，四，五，六，七，八，九，十。

teaching_notes: Start lesson 03 by reusing lesson 01-02 pronouns before introducing any new verb; the point is that the frame stays stable while the verb changes.
Keep 想 drills practical: ask only about tea, coffee, water, or food that the learner can answer immediately.
Use family nouns selectively. The lesson itself warns that the extended kinship tree becomes too complex too quickly.
When the learner confuses 和 and 喝, switch from meaning to tone contrast, then put both back into full sentences.
Close the session by counting aloud with body rhythm or finger taps so the number block becomes automatic instead of purely visual.

lessons.title: Lesson 04: Numbers, Weekdays, Months, and Birthdays

lesson_lines.translation_en: Lesson 04 starts by revisiting numbers before moving into dates and calendar language.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: shi er, shi si, shi wu

lesson_lines.translation_en: Once the class reaches twenty, the teacher turns the counting drill into a fast wake-up exercise.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: er shi yi dao san shi
san shi yi dao si shi
jiu shi yi dao yi bai

lesson_lines.translation_en: Chinese month names are built by taking a number and adding 月.
Years are also easy to say in Chinese because each digit is read separately.
Weekdays run by number just like the months do, except for Sunday.
The lesson now turns from isolated calendar words to real date questions.
几 is used when the answer will be a number rather than a noun like what or who.
The teacher explicitly contrasts 几 with 什么: date questions expect a number in the answer.
The same number-question word is reused when asking the time.
Years are spoken as a string of digits followed by 年.
The next time words are the four most common everyday anchors: yesterday, today, tomorrow, and now.
Literally, 什么时候 is built from 'what' plus a time-period word.
A normal time expression comes after the subject and before the rest of the action.
The lesson then applies time-order rules to birthday questions.
When giving a birthday date, the time expression still comes early in the sentence.
Chinese date order moves from larger to smaller units.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 你朋友什么时候过生日？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni pengyou shenme shihou guo shengri?
lesson_lines.translation_en: When does your friend celebrate their birthday?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Когда у твоего друга день рождения?

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 四月四号 / 四月五号
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: si yue si hao / si yue wu hao

lesson_lines.translation_en: The teacher confirms there are two workable birthday-question constructions in this lesson.
When is the birthday / when do they celebrate the birthday?
The date-order rule now becomes a clock-time rule.
I drink coffee at ten o'clock.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я пью кофе в десять часов.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 你朋友什么时候喝咖啡？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni pengyou shenme shihou he kafei?
lesson_lines.translation_en: What time does your friend drink coffee?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Во сколько твой друг пьёт кофе?

lesson_lines.translation_en: What do you like to drink?

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 你姐姐什么时候过生日？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni jiejie shenme shihou guo shengri?
lesson_lines.translation_en: When is your older sister's birthday?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Когда день рождения у твоей старшей сестры?

lesson_lines.translation_en: The lesson closes by turning Monday into a meeting phrase.
The farewell sequence now alternates goodbye, see you tomorrow, and see you on Monday.

grammar_patterns.name: Tens are built with numeral plus 十
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 04 extends counting by stacking a numeral with 十, then adding another numeral if needed.

grammar_patterns.name: Months are number plus 月
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Chinese month names are transparent: the numeral comes first, then 月.

grammar_patterns.name: Weekdays use 星期 plus a number
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Most weekdays are built from 星期 and a numeral, while Sunday uses 天 instead of a numeral.

grammar_patterns.name: Dates use month plus 号
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: When you say a date, put the month first and the day number before 号.

grammar_patterns.name: Years are read digit by digit plus 年
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Instead of saying thousands and hundreds as in Russian, the lesson reads each year digit in sequence and adds 年.

grammar_patterns.name: Time words come after the subject
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The class is reminded that ordinary time expressions like yesterday, now, or at ten o'clock come after the subject and before the main verb phrase.

grammar_patterns.name: Birthday questions with 什么时候
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use 什么时候 to ask when a birthday happens or when someone celebrates it.
examples: 你朋友什么时候过生日？
你姐姐什么时候过生日？

grammar_patterns.name: Clock time uses numeral plus 点
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: For simple whole-hour time expressions, use the number before 点.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: The lesson explicitly contrasts 号 for dates with 好 for good, so the learner should practice them as a tone pair, not as one memorized spelling.
Keep 月 compact with the fronted yue vowel; do not flatten it into a Russian-style ue sequence.
Weekday phrases should stay rhythmic as one unit; do not over-stress the middle syllables.
Practice 点 as the clock-time marker inside a full phrase such as 十点 or 八点吃饭.
Keep 什么时候 fluent as a single question chunk; beginners often break it into too many stressed pieces.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: What month and what date is it?
coaching_notes: Use 几 because the answer is a number.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: When is your birthday?
coaching_notes: Keep the time question after the birthday phrase.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I drink coffee at ten o'clock.
coaching_notes: Put the time expression after 我 and before the action.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: April fourth.
coaching_notes: Month first, then the day number, then 号.

drills.prompt: Count by tens from twenty to one hundred.
drills.target_answer: 二十，三十，四十，五十，六十，七十，八十，九十，一百。
coaching_notes: Keep the repeated 十 rhythm stable as the leading numeral changes.

tests.question: What does 星期一 mean?
Translate into Chinese: See you tomorrow.
Which word in lesson 04 means zero?
Translate into Chinese: My older sister's birthday is September fourth.
tests.answer_key: 我姐姐的生日是九月四号。

tests.question: Say the time phrase: I eat at eight o'clock.

teaching_notes: Keep lesson 04 practical by tying every number to a date, month, weekday, year, or clock time instead of chanting numbers in isolation for too long.
Use the birthday block as the main speaking loop: ask month, day, and year only after the learner can already ask when the birthday is.
Whenever the learner forgets word order, return to the simple template: subject, then time, then action.
Contrast 号 and 好 out loud early; the lesson itself highlights this because beginners confuse them immediately.
End the session with a real scheduling exchange such as 明天见 or 星期一见 so the calendar vocabulary becomes social, not just mechanical.

lessons.title: Lesson 05: Larger Numbers, Direction, and Going Somewhere

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 你们好吗？你们好吗？今天好吗？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni men hao ma? ni men hao ma? jintian hao ma?
lesson_lines.translation_en: How are you all? Are you all well? Is today going well?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Как вы? У вас всё хорошо? Сегодня всё хорошо?

lesson_lines.translation_en: The teacher contrasts 很好 with a stronger form, 非常好.
Lesson 05 resumes from counting to one hundred and then pushes further.
The class now moves directly from one hundred toward one thousand.
To build hundreds, put a numeral before 百.
ten in the full counting form used inside larger numbers
lesson_lines.translation_ru: десять в полной форме внутри больших чисел

lesson_lines.translation_en: For phone numbers, apartment numbers, or years, Chinese often reads the digits one by one instead of using quantity structure.
In casual speech, the middle 十 may be dropped in one-hundred-ten patterns, though the teacher advises not relying on that shortcut.
Complex numbers sometimes need an explicit zero to preserve the place value.
With 零 in place, the class can keep hundreds unambiguous.
The lesson pauses on the tone difference between 千 thousand and 钱 money.
The teacher notes that Chinese uses fewer prepositions than Russian, but the key ones still matter.
The lesson now isolates the two core motion verbs.
A small but crucial particle now marks that an action has already been completed.
Without 了, the same motion verb can stay present or future-looking instead of clearly completed.
The teacher reminds the class of the same word-order rule: subject, then time, then action, then destination.
The class now tries to say: I want to go to China.
I want to go to China.
I want to go to Beijing.
The next movement verb is the one used for returning.
The same word also carries the meaning of home, which is why it fits return-home patterns.
The teacher distinguishes returning home from returning back here.
The lesson now shifts from movement to geography words.
The class finally adds the very common frame for this / it is.
person of China; Chinese citizen / Chinese person

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 我想学汉语，因为我想去中国。
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo xiang xue Hanyu, yinwei wo xiang qu Zhongguo.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I want to study Chinese because I want to go to China.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я хочу учить китайский, потому что хочу поехать в Китай.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 这是我的朋友。他很想学汉语，因为想在中国工作。
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: zhe shi wo de pengyou. ta hen xiang xue Hanyu, yinwei xiang zai Zhongguo gongzuo.
lesson_lines.translation_en: This is my friend. He really wants to study Chinese because he wants to work in China.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Это мой друг. Он очень хочет учить китайский, потому что хочет работать в Китае.

lesson_lines.translation_en: The simple verb 说 means to say or to speak in the raw sense of speaking.
cannot; do not know how to

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo dong Zhongwen, danshi bu hui shuo.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I understand Chinese, but I cannot speak it.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я понимаю китайский, но не умею на нём говорить.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni qu guo na ge guojia?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Which country have you been to?

lesson_lines.translation_ru: На каком языке ты умеешь говорить?

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni xiang qu Zhongguo chengshi ma?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Do you want to go to a Chinese city?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Ты хочешь поехать в китайский город?

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 你喜欢俄罗斯的哪个城市？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni xihuan Eluosi de na ge chengshi?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Which city in Russia do you like?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Какой город в России тебе нравится?

lesson_lines.translation_en: I go home at eight o'clock.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я иду домой в восемь часов.
На каком языке ты умеешь говорить?

lesson_lines.translation_en: The recap boils the lesson down to countries, cities, movement verbs, and speaking languages.
The teacher closes by telling the class to digest the material and return tomorrow.

grammar_patterns.name: Hundreds and thousands use 百 and 千
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 05 extends counting by stacking numeral units such as 百 and 千 after the leading number.

grammar_patterns.name: 零 fills missing place values
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: When a complex number skips a place value, 零 keeps the structure clear, as in one hundred one.

grammar_patterns.name: Directional verbs 去 and 来
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Use 去 for movement away from the speaker and 来 for movement toward the speaker.

grammar_patterns.name: Completed action with 了
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Add 了 when the lesson wants to show that a motion event has already happened.

grammar_patterns.name: Subject, time, action, destination
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson keeps returning to the same order: subject first, then time, then the action, then where the movement goes.

grammar_patterns.name: Like pattern with 喜欢
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To say you like a city or language, put 喜欢 directly before the place or thing with no extra particle.

grammar_patterns.name: This-is frame with 这 and 这是
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson introduces a simple identifying frame: 这 for this, 这是 for this is, and then question forms from it.

grammar_patterns.name: 会说 plus a language
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To say that someone can speak a language, use 会 before 说 and then name the language.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: The lesson pairs 千 and 钱 to show that the syllable stays similar while the tone changes the meaning completely.
Keep the second syllable of 喜欢 lighter and less stressed; Russian speakers often over-hit the end of the word.
Practice 会说 as one smooth chunk because the lesson uses it as the core language-ability frame.
Keep 城市 rhythmic as a two-syllable noun rather than giving both syllables equal Russian-style weight.
Distinguish 回来 from 回家 by keeping the second syllable clear; one is back here, the other is back home.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I want to go to China.
coaching_notes: Use 想 before 去 and put the destination after the motion verb.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I like Moscow.
coaching_notes: Do not add an extra particle between 喜欢 and the city name.

drills.prompt: Ask in Chinese: What is this?
coaching_notes: Keep the word order literal: this is what.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I understand Chinese, but I cannot speak it.
coaching_notes: Use 懂 for understanding and 不会说 for inability to speak.

drills.prompt: Repeat the number pattern: one hundred one; one hundred ten; one thousand.
coaching_notes: Keep 零 audible inside one hundred one.

tests.question: What does 喜欢 mean?
Translate into Chinese: Which country have you been to?
Which word in lesson 05 means thousand?
Translate into Chinese: What language can you speak?
Say in Chinese: I go home at eight o'clock.

teaching_notes: Do not let lesson 05 become only a number chant. Tie the hundred and thousand patterns immediately to phone numbers, dates, or place counts.
The movement block is the real hinge of the lesson: contrast 去, 来, 回, 回来, and 回家 until the learner can feel the direction without translation.
Use 喜欢 plus cities or countries for natural conversational output instead of abstract geography quizzes.
When the learner reaches language names, switch from bare 说 to 会说 so ability and action stay distinct.
End on one real personal sentence with time and motion, such as 我八点回家 or 我明天去中国, to reinforce the subject-time-action pattern.

lessons.title: Lesson 06: Time of Day, Knowing, and Speaking Languages

lesson_lines.translation_en: Lesson 06 mainly consolidates earlier material and adds only a few highly useful new words.
The teacher shifts from present-time talk toward words that help describe what already happened or will happen.
By adding 上 to the time roots, the lesson builds the everyday words for morning and evening.
The class immediately uses 晚上 inside a real return-home question.
not to know; I do not know
The lesson now returns to the important two-word structure for speaking a language.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo hui shuo yi dianr Hanyu.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I can speak a little Chinese.

lesson_lines.translation_ru: На каком языке ты умеешь говорить?

lesson_lines.translation_en: I understood; now I get it.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 我不会说汉语，我学习汉语。
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo bu hui shuo Hanyu, wo xuexi Hanyu.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I cannot speak Chinese; I study Chinese.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я не говорю по-китайски; я изучаю китайский.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo bu hui shuo Hanyu, wo mingbai.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I do not speak Chinese, but I understand.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я не говорю по-китайски, но понимаю.

lesson_lines.translation_en: to speak / to be able to speak
The teacher compresses the cluster into four core verbs and states: speak, can speak, understand, and study.
What did you do yesterday evening?
Once the time phrase yesterday evening is explicit, the class treats the extra completion particle as unnecessary in this question frame.
The teacher contrasts 了 and 过: 了 marks a changed or completed event, while 过 marks the bare fact of experience.
not in past contexts / not have
lesson_lines.translation_ru: не в прошедшем времени / не иметь
Я не ходил; я не ездил.

lesson_lines.translation_en: not in present or habitual contexts
lesson_lines.translation_ru: не в настоящем или обычном контексте

lesson_lines.translation_en: Lesson 06 makes the contrast explicit: 不 for the present frame, 没 for the past frame.
The teacher resets the class with a real past-time speaking prompt about the previous evening.
Again, the class keeps the sentence anchored with the full time phrase 昨天晚上.
I did not study yesterday evening.
What are you doing this evening?
The teacher points out that time-of-day greetings are natural variants of ordinary greetings.
What are we going to do this evening?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Что мы будем делать сегодня вечером?

lesson_lines.translation_en: The class improvises an evening plan and uses the known verbs for drinking and sleeping practice.
Have you eaten? / Have you had your meal?
The teacher turns a cluster of daily routines into verb-object chunks: eat a meal, drink tea, and sleep.
you are welcome; don't mention it
A native-speaking colleague joins so the class can answer spontaneous questions with the recycled lesson material.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni jintian wanshang xiang zuo shenme?
lesson_lines.translation_en: What do you want to do this evening?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Что ты хочешь делать сегодня вечером?

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni xihuan chi Zhongguo cai ma?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Do you like eating Chinese food?

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni hui zuo Zhongguo cai ma?

lesson_lines.translation_en: The teacher warns that without naming the cuisine, the sentence can sound as if the learner cannot cook anything at all.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 明天晚上你想在哪儿吃饭？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: mingtian wanshang ni xiang zai nar chi fan?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Where do you want to eat tomorrow evening?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Где ты хочешь поесть завтра вечером?

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo bu zhidao zhe shi shenme.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I do not know what this is.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 昨天你做什么了？我昨天工作了。
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: zuotian ni zuo shenme le? wo zuotian gongzuo le.
lesson_lines.translation_en: What did you do yesterday? I worked yesterday.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Что ты делал вчера? Я вчера работал.

lesson_lines.translation_en: The food drill ends with a successful full phrase for Chinese cuisine and an explicit teacher approval.
The teacher notes that the separate fragments are finally starting to crystallize into a usable system.

vocabulary.translation_en: not know; I do not know
not in present or habitual contexts

grammar_patterns.name: Time roots become parts of day with 上
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 06 builds morning and evening by taking simple time roots and combining them into 早上 and 晚上.

grammar_patterns.name: 知道 and 吗 build a practical check-in question
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The class turns 知道 into a dialogue tool by asking 你知道吗 and answering with 知道 or 不知道.

grammar_patterns.name: 会说 plus a language
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To express language ability, lesson 06 keeps using 会 before 说 and then adds the language name.

grammar_patterns.name: 明白了 marks a completed change of understanding
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: When the learner has just understood something, 了 marks that change of state, as in 我明白了.

grammar_patterns.name: 不 versus 没 for present and past negation
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson explicitly contrasts 不 for current or habitual negation and 没 for past actions that did not happen.

grammar_patterns.name: Explicit time phrases can carry past meaning without extra 了
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: When yesterday evening is already named, the time phrase itself anchors the sentence in the past and the teacher avoids forcing another completion particle into the question.

grammar_patterns.name: Verb-object daily routine chunks
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Daily actions appear as tight verb-object units that should be learned whole: 吃饭, 喝茶, 睡觉, and 做饭.

grammar_patterns.name: Social repair formulas use fixed response pairs
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 06 adds useful interaction pairs: 对不起 for apology, 没关系 for reassurance, and 不客气 as the reply to 谢谢.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Keep 当然 as a smooth two-syllable chunk; Russian speakers often over-separate the second syllable.
Practice 早上 and 晚上 as time-of-day bundles rather than stressing each character like a list.
Do not flatten the final particle in 明白了; the sentence should still sound like a completed realization rather than a plain dictionary form.
Treat 对不起 as one apology formula. Beginners often pause after 对, which makes it sound fragmented.
Keep 不客气 light and brisk as a response formula, not as three isolated dictionary words.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: Do you know?
coaching_notes: Use 知道 plus the question particle 吗.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I cannot speak Chinese; I study Chinese.
drills.target_answer: 我不会说汉语。我学习汉语。
coaching_notes: Keep the contrast clean: negative language ability first, then the study verb.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: What did you do yesterday evening?
coaching_notes: Place the time phrase before the main verb.

drills.prompt: Repeat the greeting pair: good morning / good evening.
coaching_notes: Keep each greeting as a fixed chunk.

drills.prompt: Roleplay the food exchange: ask whether someone likes Chinese cuisine and whether they can cook it.
drills.target_answer: 你喜欢吃中国菜吗？你会做中国菜吗？
coaching_notes: First ask about liking, then ask about cooking ability.

tests.question: What does 当然 mean?
Translate into Chinese: I do not know.
Which negative particle does lesson 06 use for past actions that did not happen?
Say the cultural check-in phrase: Have you eaten?
Translate into Chinese: Sorry. It is okay. You are welcome.

teaching_notes: Keep lesson 06 practical by returning to already-known verbs and only then layering in new time words and social formulas.
Use the contrast between 不 and 没 as the main grammar loop; it is the most useful new distinction in this lesson.
Do not teach 吃饭, 喝茶, 睡觉, and 做饭 as isolated verbs. Keep them as whole action chunks.
The native-speaker drill is best used after the learner can already answer three safe questions: 你知道吗, 你今天晚上做什么, and 你喜欢吃中国菜吗.
End the session with apology and thanks responses so the learner leaves with polite survival language, not only classroom grammar.

lessons.title: Lesson 07: How, Can, and Time-Sensitive Questions

lesson_lines.translation_en: Lesson 07 opens by adding one more useful question word so the class can stop recycling the same few prompts.
How are you now? How are things right now?
not so well; so-so; not great
The teacher states the core point directly: Chinese does not really mark tense as a grammatical category the way Russian does.
If a sentence already names yesterday, today, tomorrow, or now, the time reference is usually clear without extra markers.
to look; to watch; to read
I do not watch / I do not look.
Am I watching? / Do I watch?
I watched it; I have watched it with a completed result.
I have watched it before; I have the experience.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Я смотрел; у меня был такой опыт.

lesson_lines.translation_en: Did I watch it? Have I watched it?
The lesson now switches from completed-result meaning to the bare fact of having had an experience.
experiential particle; having done something before
I am going to watch; I intend to watch.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 爱 / 恨 / 希望 / 喜欢
lesson_lines.translation_en: to love / to hate / to hope / to like
The teacher argues that feeling verbs such as love or like are treated as outside these time markers in ordinary usage.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni chi guo Zhongguo cai ma?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Have you ever eaten Chinese food?
The teacher explicitly says that 吃了吗 can function as a real greeting more often than a literal food question.
With 了, the marker may appear right after the verb in forms like 吃了 or 看了.
By contrast, 过 sits only directly after the verb.
The lesson then introduces a very literal expression for visiting friends.
to go see friends; to visit friends
If the sentence already says yesterday, the time reference is already anchored.
I went to visit friends yesterday.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni qu kan pengyou le ma?

lesson_lines.translation_en: to eat breakfast / to eat dinner
The teacher condenses the core Chinese order into a simple working template.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 主语 + 时间 + 动作 + 对象
主语 + 时间 + 地点 + 动作 + 对象
你喜欢在家吃饭还是在饭馆吃饭？
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni xihuan zai jia chi fan haishi zai fanguan chi fan?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Do you like eating at home or in a restaurant?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Ты любишь есть дома или в ресторане?

lesson_lines.translation_en: What movies do you like to watch?
to watch what kind of movie
to read, when the object is a book
I did not catch sight of her; I did not manage to see her.
to see; to catch sight of

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni jintian shenme shihou hui jia?
lesson_lines.translation_en: When are you going home today?

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo de gongzuo ye hen hao.
lesson_lines.translation_en: My work is also very good.
lesson_lines.translation_ru: У меня на работе тоже всё хорошо.

lesson_lines.translation_en: I do not like drinking tea.
I drink coffee in the morning.
What do you want to do in the evening?

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 美国电影 / 意大利电影

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: wo xihuan zai jia kan dianying.
lesson_lines.translation_en: I like watching films at home.
The teacher closes by telling the class to recombine the verb pattern with the words they already know and come back tomorrow.

vocabulary.translation_en: to look; to watch; to read
to be going to; to intend to

vocabulary.zh_hanzi: 爱 / 恨 / 希望 / 喜欢
vocabulary.translation_en: to love / to hate / to hope / to like
to eat breakfast / to eat dinner
to see; to catch sight of

vocabulary.zh_hanzi: 美国电影 / 意大利电影

vocabulary.zh_pinyin: wo xihuan zai jia kan dianying.
vocabulary.translation_en: I like watching films at home.

grammar_patterns.name: 怎么 asks how
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 07 adds 怎么 so the learner can ask how someone is or how something went instead of repeating only what and who questions.

grammar_patterns.name: 可以 marks possibility or permission
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The class contrasts 可以 with 会: 可以 means can or may in the sense of possibility or permission, not learned ability.

grammar_patterns.name: Context often carries time in Chinese
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: If the sentence already names yesterday, today, tomorrow, or now, the teacher treats the time reference as sufficiently established without extra tense marking.

grammar_patterns.name: Verb matrix: plain, negated, completed, experiential, and future-intention forms
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The core board work is a verb matrix around 看: plain 我看, negated 我不看, question 我看吗, completed 我看了, experiential 我看过, past negation 我没看, never-experienced 我没看过, and future intention 我要看. The lesson also says future can often be carried by context such as tomorrow.

grammar_patterns.name: 了 versus 过
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: 了 marks a completed result or changed state, while 过 marks the fact of having had an experience before.

grammar_patterns.name: 没 negates past events
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To say that something did not happen or has never happened, lesson 07 uses 没 instead of 不.

grammar_patterns.name: 要 marks intention
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: If the teacher wants to highlight future intention rather than just rely on context, 要 is used as a marker of intention or plan.

grammar_patterns.name: Chinese order: subject, time, place, action, object
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson states the working sentence template explicitly and keeps reusing it in food and daily-routine drills.
examples: 主语 + 时间 + 动作 + 对象
主语 + 时间 + 地点 + 动作 + 对象

grammar_patterns.name: 还是 builds either-or questions
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: When the learner must choose between two alternatives, 还是 links the options inside one question.
examples: 你喜欢在家吃饭还是在饭馆吃饭？
中国电影还是美国电影？

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Keep 怎么 and 怎么样 flowing as compact question chunks; beginners often over-separate them syllable by syllable.
Do not confuse 可以 with 会. In this lesson 可以 should sound like a permission-or-possibility chunk, not like the ability verb from earlier lessons.
Practice 看了 and 看过 back to back so the learner hears the different aspect meaning while keeping the verb stem stable.
Treat 吃了吗 as a natural greeting chunk instead of stressing every syllable equally.
Keep 还是 light and connective inside a choice question rather than turning it into a heavily stressed standalone word.
examples: 在家吃饭还是在饭馆吃饭

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: How are you now?
coaching_notes: Use 怎么样 as one question chunk.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: Can you come today?
coaching_notes: Use 可以 for possibility, not 会.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I have never seen it.
coaching_notes: Use 没 with 过 for never having had the experience.

drills.prompt: Repeat the greeting pair: Have you eaten? I have eaten.
coaching_notes: Keep both meal expressions as fixed social chunks.

drills.prompt: Roleplay the choice question: ask whether someone prefers eating at home or in a restaurant.
drills.target_answer: 你喜欢在家吃饭还是在饭馆吃饭？
coaching_notes: Use 还是 to connect the two options.

tests.question: What does 还可以 mean in this lesson?
Translate into Chinese: Have you been to China?
Which particle in lesson 07 marks bare experience rather than a completed result?
Say the sentence: I went to visit friends yesterday.
Translate into Chinese: I do not like drinking tea.

teaching_notes: Keep lesson 07 anchored in the Petrov-inspired verb matrix. The main value is not individual words alone, but the contrast between plain, completed, experiential, future/intention, and negated forms.
Use 看 as the main demo verb first, then swap in other known verbs like 去, 吃, and 喝 only after the pattern feels stable.
When learners over-focus on tense, return them to the lesson's central idea: Chinese often lets the time phrase and context carry the time reference.
Teach 吃了吗 and 还可以 as social chunks, not as purely analytical grammar examples. They make the learner sound natural immediately.
Save the movie-country drill for the end of the session; it is a good way to recycle 还是, 看电影, and country adjectives after the heavier grammar block.

lessons.title: Lesson 08: Meeting People, Age, and Quantity Questions

lesson_lines.translation_en: Lesson 08 expands the politeness and social-expression layer before moving into more grammar and routine speech.
The teacher introduces 高兴 as the basic way to say glad or happy.
to know, in the sense of having information
to know someone; to be acquainted with; to get acquainted
to know someone; to meet and become acquainted
I am glad to meet you.
The lesson uses 高兴认识你 as a standard polite self-introduction formula.
Do you know him? Are you acquainted with him?
Age statements use 岁 directly, with no extra verb like 'to be' or 'to have'.
The teacher builds 'how much' by combining the words for much and little into 多少.
How old are you? Literally: how big are you?
lesson_lines.translation_ru: Сколько тебе лет? Буквально: насколько ты большой?

lesson_lines.translation_en: For children, the lesson uses a direct age question rather than the more adult quantity pattern.
How old are you? for a child
to get up; to get out of bed
When do you go to sleep?

The class now shifts into a daily-routine retelling exercise to recycle the new verbs in sequence.

grammar_patterns.name: 认识 versus 知道
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 08 distinguishes knowing information from knowing a person. 知道 is informational knowledge, while 认识 is acquaintance or getting acquainted.

grammar_patterns.name: Age uses 岁, not 年
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To say how old a person is, Chinese uses 岁 directly. The teacher explicitly contrasts this with 年 as a year number.

grammar_patterns.name: Quantity question with 多少
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The class builds 'how much / how many' by merging the words for much and little into 多少.

grammar_patterns.name: Age question with 多大
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: For a general age question, lesson 08 uses 多大, literally 'how big'.

grammar_patterns.name: Result complements with 到
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Adding 到 turns basic action verbs into result forms such as seeing, hearing, or finding successfully.

grammar_patterns.name: Clause linking with 的时候
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: 的时候 links a whole clause to mean 'when' or 'at the time when' rather than asking a direct question.

grammar_patterns.name: Shopping phrase 买东西
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson packages shopping as a whole action phrase, 买东西, instead of forcing a single special verb for 'to shop'.

grammar_patterns.name: Routine order stays subject, time, action
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The daily-routine drill keeps the same broad Chinese order: who, then when, then what action happens.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Treat 高兴认识你 as a smooth social chunk. If you pause after each syllable, it stops sounding like a greeting.
Keep 多少 and 多大 distinct: both start with 多, but the second syllable changes the meaning completely.
Do not clip 告诉 into separate dictionary syllables. It should feel like one quick command chunk in 请告诉我.
Practice 睡觉 and 起床 as daily-routine units; they sound more natural as ready-made actions than as decomposed characters.
的时候 should sound like a clause linker, not like a stop-start string of isolated syllables.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: I am glad to meet you.
coaching_notes: Keep 高兴认识你 as one polite greeting chunk.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: How old are you?
coaching_notes: For a general adult question, use 多大.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: Please tell me something.
coaching_notes: The core target is 告诉我; the exact object can vary in live speech.

drills.prompt: Repeat the routine pair: Did you eat breakfast? I ate breakfast.
drills.target_answer: 你吃早饭了吗？我吃了早饭。
coaching_notes: Keep both lines as stable routine chunks.

drills.prompt: Roleplay the shopping question: ask where someone buys things in Beijing.
drills.target_answer: 你在哪儿买东西？在北京买东西。
coaching_notes: Keep 买东西 as one shopping phrase.

tests.question: What does 高兴 mean?
Translate into Chinese: I do not have Chinese friends.
Which expression in lesson 08 means 'when; at the time when' as a clause linker?
Say the sentence: When I am in China, I eat Chinese food.
tests.answer_key: 在中国的时候，我吃中国菜。

tests.question: Translate into Chinese: I like reading in the evening.

teaching_notes: Keep lesson 08 social first. 高兴认识你 and the two 'know' verbs immediately make the learner sound more natural in conversation.
Use age and quantity together in one block. 岁, 多少, and 多大 are easier to retain when contrasted directly.
Treat result-complement pairs as upgrades of familiar verbs: 看 becomes 看到, 听 becomes 听到, 找 becomes 找到.
When introducing 的时候, insist that it links a whole clause. Do not let the learner treat it as just another version of a direct time question.
Use the late shopping and leisure drills to move the learner away from only work-and-study sentences into more personal conversation.

lessons.title: Lesson 09: Greetings, Demonstratives, Classifiers, and Comparison

lesson_lines.translation_en: Lesson 09 opens by reviewing etiquette formulas before moving into classifier words and descriptive language.
The teacher introduces classifier words as the next structural layer after numbers and demonstratives.
Give me a cup of coffee.
mouth; classifier used for family members
lesson_lines.translation_ru: рот; счётное слово для членов семьи

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni jia you ji kou ren?

The lesson now shifts from classifiers to adjectives and comparison.
Coffee is more expensive than tea.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: zhe ge shangdian bi na ge gui.
lesson_lines.translation_en: This store is more expensive than that one.

lesson_lines.zh_hanzi: 最好 / 最贵 / 最难
lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: zui hao / zui gui / zui nan
lesson_lines.translation_ru: самый лучший / самый дорогой / самый трудный

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: zhe ge bi na ge hao.
lesson_lines.translation_en: This one is better than that one.
to think; to feel; to consider
The class now turns to colors and how to attach them to nouns.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni de che shi shenme yanse?

vocabulary.zh_pinyin: ni jia you ji kou ren?

Can it be a little cheaper?

grammar_patterns.name: Demonstratives and numbers need a classifier
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: After numbers and words like 这 and 那, Chinese normally inserts a classifier before the noun. Lesson 09 drills this with 个.

grammar_patterns.name: 个 is the default classifier, but some nouns use more specific ones
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The class treats 个 as the safe default, then contrasts it with 杯 for drinks and 口 for family members.

grammar_patterns.name: Comparison with 比
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To say one thing is more X than another, lesson 09 uses A 比 B + adjective.

grammar_patterns.name: Too much with 太 + adjective
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: For shopping and reaction, 太 before an adjective means 'too' or 'so' in the strong sense, as in 太贵.

grammar_patterns.name: Superlative with 最
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: 最 turns an adjective into the superlative: best, most expensive, most difficult.

grammar_patterns.name: Opinion frame with 我觉得
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson introduces 觉得 as the way to frame a personal opinion before a comparison or judgment.
examples: 我觉得红茶比绿茶好喝。

grammar_patterns.name: Asking and describing color
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 09 asks color with 什么颜色 and then turns color words into noun modifiers with 色 and 的.
examples: 你喜欢什么颜色的衣服？

grammar_patterns.name: Choice question with 还是
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: When offering alternatives, the class uses 还是 between two choices, especially in drink and shopping comparisons.
examples: 中国的东西便宜还是俄罗斯的东西便宜

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Keep 好久不见 as one social chunk. The lesson explicitly contrasts 久 'long time' with 九 'nine', so tone clarity matters.

pronunciation_focus.topic: 个, 杯, and 干杯
pronunciation_focus.coaching: Do not blur 个 and 杯 into the same vowel sound. 干杯 should sound like a toast chunk, not two dictionary syllables.
The teacher highlights the difference between 绿 and 六. Keep the green-color word distinct from the number six.
颜色 should flow as a stable question chunk in 什么颜色 instead of sounding like two unrelated syllables.
便宜 is useful but easy to flatten. Keep it quick and light so bargaining lines stay natural.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: Long time no see.
coaching_notes: Treat it as one etiquette chunk, not four isolated syllables.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: This person loves you.
coaching_notes: Keep 个 between the demonstrative and the noun.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: Give me a cup of coffee.
coaching_notes: Use 杯 for a drink and keep the full request fluent.

drills.prompt: Ask in Chinese: What color clothes do you like?
drills.target_answer: 你喜欢什么颜色的衣服？
coaching_notes: Use 什么颜色 for the question and 的 to attach the color to 衣服.

drills.prompt: Roleplay the bargaining line: This is too expensive. Can it be a little cheaper?
drills.target_answer: 这个太贵。可以便宜一点吗？
coaching_notes: This is the lesson's most practical shopping line. Keep it direct.

tests.question: Which classifier does lesson 09 teach as the safe default for many nouns?
Which classifier is used for drinks in this lesson?
Translate into Chinese: Coffee is more expensive than tea.
How do you ask 'what color?' in lesson 09?
Say the sentence: I think red tea tastes better than green tea.
tests.answer_key: 我觉得红茶比绿茶好喝。

teaching_notes: Do not rush past the opening etiquette review. 好久不见 gives the learner a more natural social entry point than repeating only 你好.
Teach classifiers as a structural habit, not a list to memorize in isolation: 个 first, then 杯 and 口 as memorable exceptions.
Use 比, 太, and 最 together so the learner feels one comparison system rather than three disconnected words.
In the color block, contrast asking the color with attaching the color to a noun. 什么颜色 and 红色的衣服 should not collapse into one pattern in the learner's mind.
Finish with the shopping lines. 这个太贵 and 可以便宜一点吗 are high-value survival phrases and make the lesson immediately usable.

two as a number / two used before many nouns

vocabulary.translation_en: Do you think Chinese or English is more difficult?
vocabulary.translation_ru: Как ты думаешь, китайский или английский сложнее?

vocabulary.translation_en: What clothes are you wearing today?
today I am wearing blue clothes
What color do you like most?
vocabulary.translation_ru: Какой цвет тебе нравится больше всего?

grammar_patterns.name: Clothing often takes specific classifiers, not just 个
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 10 repeats that 个 is understandable but not always idiomatic. Clothes and shoes often need more specific classifiers such as 件 and 双.

grammar_patterns.name: Long items use 条
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: For long or elongated items such as trousers, lesson 10 uses 条 rather than the default classifier.

grammar_patterns.name: Color attaches to nouns through 色的 or direct modifier position
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The teacher shows how to attach a color to a clothing item or a car rather than naming the color in isolation.

grammar_patterns.name: Degree adjustment with 一点
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: A little more or a little less is built with 一点, including the practical request to speak more slowly.

grammar_patterns.name: Comparison with 快 and 慢
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson reuses comparison language with fast and slow, including car-speed comparisons.

grammar_patterns.name: Two as 二 versus 两
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: Lesson 10 distinguishes bare number two from the form used before many nouns, paired items, and grouped objects.

grammar_patterns.name: Driving versus riding
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: To drive, the class uses 开车. To go by vehicle as a passenger, it uses 坐车.

grammar_patterns.name: Question frame for what someone is wearing
grammar_patterns.explanation_en: The lesson packages clothing talk into a ready-made conversational question with 穿 and 今天.

pronunciation_focus.coaching: Keep the clothing classifiers distinct. If you blur them together, the clothing block collapses into noise.
穿 should stay short and stable in clothing sentences. It is one of the central verbs of the lesson.
蓝色的 works best as one flowing color-modifier chunk, especially when attached to 车 or 衣服.
This is a high-value survival phrase. Keep it compact and polite rather than pronouncing each word as a separate dictionary entry.
Do not merge the initial consonants: 开车 and 坐车 must stay distinct because they encode different roles in the same vehicle situation.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: Today I am wearing blue clothes.
coaching_notes: Keep 穿 as the main clothing verb and put 今天 before it.

drills.prompt: Say in Chinese: one shirt, one pair of shoes, one pair of trousers.
drills.target_answer: 一件衬衫，一双鞋，一条裤子。
coaching_notes: This is the core classifier drill of lesson 10.

drills.prompt: Ask in Chinese: What color is your car?
coaching_notes: Use the full color question, not only the bare noun color.

drills.prompt: Repeat the survival phrase: Please speak a little more slowly.
coaching_notes: This line should become automatic.

drills.prompt: Roleplay the transport contrast: say that you do not drive, you go by car as a passenger.
coaching_notes: The lesson's key contrast is 开车 versus 坐车.

tests.question: Which classifier does lesson 10 use for a shirt?
Which classifier does lesson 10 use for shoes?
Translate into Chinese: My car is blue.
Which pair in lesson 10 means to drive versus to ride as a passenger?
Say the sentence: What clothes are you wearing today?

teaching_notes: Do not let lesson 10 turn into a word list. Keep classifiers and clothing nouns bound together as one operational system.
Use 件, 双, and 条 as the memorable skeleton of the lesson. That gives the learner a practical reason to care about measure words.
Anchor the color material in real objects: shirt, clothes, and car. This is what turns lesson 09 color words into useful speech.
Teach 请说慢一点 as a survival phrase, not a grammar example. It directly helps the learner keep a live conversation going.
Close with 开车 versus 坐车 so the learner leaves with a useful everyday distinction instead of only clothing vocabulary.

lessons.title: Lesson 11: Restaurants, Food Choice, and Preference Language

lesson_lines.translation_en: Lesson 11 finally moves into the food topic that earlier lessons only touched through tea, coffee, water, and rice.
The teacher explicitly explains 馆 as a productive element for public establishments.

lesson_lines.translation_en: Food and alcohol roots are used to explain how Chinese venue words for restaurants and hotels historically formed.
The class is told that these venue roots can blur the border between restaurant and hotel vocabulary.
The teacher introduces Peking duck as a culturally iconic food item that any beginner should recognize.
The class reuses 比 to compare tastes and build stronger preference language.
The teacher glosses 比较喜欢 as liking something more than the alternatives.

lesson_lines.zh_pinyin: ni bijiao xihuan rou haishi yu?
lesson_lines.translation_en: Do you prefer meat or fish?
After the meat compounds, the teacher points out that the cow also gives milk.

do not add sugar; no sugar needed
